informal: Let $q \in \mathbb{Z}$ be a prime with $q \equiv 3 \bmod 4$. Prove that the quotient ring $\mathbb{Z}[i] /(q)$ is a field with $q^{2}$ elements.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_8_3_6b_corrected {q : ℕ} (hq0 : q.Prime) (hq1 : q ≡ 3 [ZMOD 4]) :
    IsField (GaussianInt ⧸ span ({↑q} : Set GaussianInt)) ∧
    ∃ finR : Fintype (GaussianInt ⧸ span ({↑q} : Set GaussianInt)),
      @Fintype.card _ finR = q ^ 2
reasoning: The Lean signature encodes q as a Nat prime with q ≡ 3 [ZMOD 4], which captures the positive integer primes congruent to 3 mod 4 (the only mathematically distinct case since the ideal (q) equals (-q) in ℤ[i]). The quotient `GaussianInt ⧸ span ({↑q} : Set GaussianInt)` is exactly ℤ[i]/(q). The conjunction `IsField ... ∧ ∃ finR : Fintype ..., Fintype.card = q^2` matches the informal claim that the quotient is a field with q² elements. The use of an existential Fintype instance is the standard way to assert finiteness with given cardinality. No extra hypotheses are added and none are dropped.
